title.zh: 卷十五 李王鄧來列傳
title.en: Volume 15: Biographies of Li, Wang, Deng, Lai
title.raw: 卷十五 李王鄧來列傳 Volume 15: Biographies of Li, Wang, Deng, Lai

sentences.zh: 李通，字次元，南陽宛人也。

translations.literal: Li Tong, styled Ciyuan, was a man of Wan in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Li Tong of Nanyang’s Wan district took the courtesy name Ciyuan.

sentences.zh: 世以貸殖著姓。

translations.literal: For generations the family was noted for a surname built on lending and trade.
translations.idiomatic: For generations the Lis had grown rich as moneylenders and merchants.

sentences.zh: 父守，身長九尺，容貌絕異，為人嚴毅，居家如官廷。

translations.literal: His father Shou, nine chi in height, with appearance surpassingly unusual, by nature stern and severe, in the home was like an official court.
translations.idiomatic: His father Shou stood nine feet tall, looked like no other man, ran his household with the discipline of a yamen, and was stern by nature.

sentences.zh: 初事劉歆，好星曆讖記，為王莽宗卿師。

translations.literal: In the beginning he served Liu Xin, loved astrology, calendars, and prophetic texts, and became director for the imperial clan under Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: He began his career under Liu Xin, dabbled in astrology and prophecy, and rose to Wang Mang’s post of director for the imperial clan.

sentences.zh: 通亦為五威將軍從事，出補巫丞，有能名。

translations.literal: Tong also served as clerk to the general of the Five Majesties, went out to fill a vacancy as assistant of Wu, and had a reputation for competence.
translations.idiomatic: Li Tong served as an aide to the Five Majesties general, then as magistrate’s assistant at Wu, where he earned a name for ability.

sentences.zh: 莽未，百姓愁怨，通素聞守說讖云『劉氏復興，李氏為輔』，私常懷之。

translations.literal: Near the end of the Mang era the common people were anxious and resentful; Tong had long heard Shou expound a prophecy saying “The Liu will rise again; the Li will be their helpers,” and privately always harbored it.
translations.idiomatic: As Wang Mang’s rule collapsed, the people groaned under it; Li Tong remembered his father’s prophecy—“Han will return, and the Li will stand at its side”—and kept it locked in his heart.

sentences.zh: 且居家富逸，為閭里雄，以此不樂為吏，乃自免歸。

translations.literal: Moreover, dwelling at home in wealth and ease, he was a champion of the lanes and hamlets; for this reason he was not pleased to be an official, and thereupon resigned of his own accord and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Rich and influential in his ward, he had no taste for petty office and resigned to live at home.

sentences.zh: 及下江、新市兵起，南陽騷動，通從弟軼，亦素好事，乃共計議曰：『今四方擾亂，新室且亡，漢當更興。

translations.literal: When the Lower Yang and Xinshi troops rose, Nanyang was in turmoil; Tong’s younger male cousin Yi, who also had long loved stirring affairs, thereupon together planned and consulted, saying, “Now the four quarters are disturbed and chaotic; the New house is about to perish; the Han ought to rise anew.
translations.idiomatic: When the Xinshi and Lower Yang rebels stirred Nanyang, Li Tong and his cousin Li Yi—another man who loved intrigue—agreed: “The realm is in chaos, Xin is finished, and Han must rise again.

sentences.zh: 南陽宗室，獨劉伯升兄弟泛愛容眾，可與謀大事。

translations.literal: Among the Nanyang imperial clan only Liu Bosheng’s brothers broadly love and embrace the multitude; with them one may plan a great affair.”
translations.idiomatic: Only Bosheng’s brothers among the Nanyang Lius have the breadth to rally men—only they are fit for a great enterprise.”

sentences.zh: 』通笑曰：『吾意也。

translations.literal: ” Tong laughed and said, “That is my intent too.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Tong smiled. “I have been thinking the same.”

sentences.zh: 』會光武避吏在宛，通聞之，即遣軼往迎光武。

translations.literal: ” It happened that Guangwu was avoiding clerical pursuit at Wan; Tong heard of it and immediately sent Yi to go welcome Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Just then Guangwu was hiding from the law in Wan; Li Tong sent Li Yi at once to fetch him.

sentences.zh: 光武初以通士君子相慕也，故往答之。

translations.literal: Guangwu at first thought that Tong, as a gentleman scholar, admired him mutually, and therefore went to answer him.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu assumed a scholar’s courtesy call and went to return the visit.

sentences.zh: 及相見，共語移日，握手極歡。

translations.literal: When they met, they spoke together until the day shifted; they clasped hands with utmost joy.
translations.idiomatic: They talked from dawn to dusk and parted only after a warm clasp of hands.

sentences.zh: 通因具言讖文事，光武初殊不意，未敢當之。

translations.literal: Tong thereupon fully set forth the matter of the prophetic text; Guangwu at first was utterly unprepared for it and did not dare accept it.
translations.idiomatic: Li Tong laid out the prophecy in full; Guangwu was taken aback and hesitated to commit.

sentences.zh: 時守在長安，光武乃微觀通曰：『即如此，當如宗卿師何？

translations.literal: At the time Shou was in Chang’an; Guangwu thereupon subtly observed Tong and said, “If it is thus, what of your father the director for the imperial clan?”
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu murmured, “Your father still serves as Mang’s director for the clan—what becomes of him?”

sentences.zh: 』通曰：『已自有度矣。

translations.literal: Tong said, “I already have my own measure.”
translations.idiomatic: “I have already reckoned with that,” said Li Tong.

sentences.zh: 』因復備言其計。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon he again fully set forth his plan.
translations.idiomatic: He then unfolded the whole scheme.

sentences.zh: 光武既深知通意，乃遂相約結，定謀議，期以材官都試騎士日，欲劫前隊大夫及屬正，因以號令大眾。

translations.literal: After Guangwu had deeply understood Tong’s intent, they thereupon mutually pledged alliance, fixed plans and counsel, and set the date on the day when the skilled-archer host would conduct its annual mounted examination, intending to seize the front-rank grandee and the adjunct rectifier and thereby issue commands to the great host.
translations.idiomatic: Once Guangwu saw his purpose, they swore brotherhood and chose the day of the annual archer muster at Wan to kidnap the front-rank grandee and his adjunct, then rally the city.

sentences.zh: 乃使光武與軼歸舂陵，舉兵以相應。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent Guangwu and Yi to return to Chunling and raise troops to answer him.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Guangwu and Li Yi to Chunling to raise the clan in support.

sentences.zh: 遣從兄子季之長安，以事報守。

translations.literal: He dispatched the son of his elder male cousin, Ji, to Chang’an to report the affair to Shou.
translations.idiomatic: He sent his cousin’s son Ji to Chang’an to warn his father.

sentences.zh: 季於道病死，守密知之，欲亡歸。

translations.literal: Ji died of illness on the road; Shou secretly learned of it and wished to flee home.
translations.idiomatic: Ji died en route; Shou guessed the truth and planned to flee.

sentences.zh: 素與邑人黃顯相善，時顯為中郎將，聞之，謂守曰：『今關門禁嚴，君狀貌非心，將以此安之？

translations.literal: He had long been on good terms with his townsman Huang Xian; at the time Xian was gentleman of the palace; hearing of it, he said to Shou, “Now the passes are barred tight; your bearing marks you as no ordinary man—how will you find safety?
translations.idiomatic: Shou’s friend Huang Xian, a gentleman of the palace, urged him: “The gates are shut; you look like a man with a secret—where could you hide?

sentences.zh: 不如詣闕自歸。

translations.literal: It would be better to go to the palace gates and surrender yourself.
translations.idiomatic: Better present yourself at court and confess.”

sentences.zh: 事既未然，脫可免禍。

translations.literal: The affair has not yet come to pass; perhaps you can escape disaster.
translations.idiomatic: The plot is not yet proven—you may yet escape ruin.”

sentences.zh: 』守從其計，即上書歸死，章未及報，留闕下。

translations.literal: ” Shou followed his plan, immediately submitted a memorial offering his life in surrender; the memorial had not yet received reply and remained below the palace gates.
translations.idiomatic: Shou took his advice, memorialized offering his life, and waited at the gate for an answer that never came.

sentences.zh: 會事發覺，通得亡走，莽聞之，乃系守於獄。

translations.literal: It happened that the affair was discovered; Tong obtained escape and fled; Mang heard of it and thereupon bound Shou in prison.
translations.idiomatic: The conspiracy leaked; Li Tong fled while Wang Mang threw Shou into chains.

sentences.zh: 而黃顯為請曰：『守聞子無狀，不敢逃亡，守義自信，歸命宮闕。

translations.literal: But Huang Xian pled for him, saying, “Shou heard that his son behaved lawlessly and did not dare flee; trusting in righteousness he returned his life to the palace gates.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Xian interceded: “Shou learned of his son’s treason yet did not run; trusting in justice he surrendered at the gates.

sentences.zh: 臣顯願質守俱東，曉說其子。

translations.literal: Your servant Xian is willing to go east together with Shou as hostage and enlighten his son.
translations.idiomatic: Let me escort him east as surety and reason with the boy.”

sentences.zh: 如遂悖逆，令守北向刎首，以謝大恩。

translations.literal: If he then acts rebelliously, order Shou to face north and cut his throat, thereby to thank the great grace.
translations.idiomatic: If the son still rebels, let Shou cut his own throat northward to repay your mercy.”

sentences.zh: 』莽然其言。

translations.literal: ” Mang approved his words.
translations.idiomatic: Mang granted the request.

sentences.zh: 會前隊復上通起兵之狀，莽怒，欲殺守，顯爭之，遂並被誅，及守家在長安者盡殺之。

translations.literal: It happened that the front-rank army again reported upward Tong’s condition of raising troops; Mang was angry and wished to kill Shou; Xian disputed it, and thereupon both were executed together, and all of Shou’s household who were at Chang’an were killed.
translations.idiomatic: Then fresh word came from the front army; Mang’s rage turned on Shou. Huang Xian argued in vain—both men were executed, and every member of Shou’s family in Chang’an died with them.

sentences.zh: 南陽亦誅通兄弟、門宗六十四人，皆焚屍宛市。

translations.literal: In Nanyang they also executed Tong’s brothers and lineage to sixty-four persons, all burning the corpses in the market of Wan.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang officials slaughtered sixty-four of the Li kindred and burned the bodies in Wan’s marketplace.

sentences.zh: 時，漢兵亦已大合。

translations.literal: At the time the Han troops had also already greatly assembled.
translations.idiomatic: By then the Han armies had massed in force.

sentences.zh: 通與光武、李軼相遇棘陽，遂共破前隊，殺甄阜、梁丘賜。

translations.literal: Tong met Guangwu and Li Yi at Jiyang, thereupon together broke the front-rank army, killing Zhen Fu and Liangqiu Ci.
translations.idiomatic: Li Tong joined Guangwu and Li Yi at Jiyang, and together they shattered the front-rank host and killed Zhen Fu and Liangqiu Ci.

sentences.zh: 通布衣唱義，助成大業，重以寧平公主故，特見親重。

translations.literal: Tong in plain cloth was first to raise the righteous cause, assisted in completing the great enterprise, and moreover because of the princess of Ningping was especially seen as intimate and weighty.
translations.idiomatic: A commoner who first lit the flame of revolt, he helped win the empire and, as father-in-law to the Princess of Ningping, stood closer to the throne than almost any other subject.

sentences.zh: 然性謙恭，常欲避權勢。

translations.literal: Yet by nature he was modest and respectful and always wished to avoid power and influence.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he was humble by nature and shunned raw power.

sentences.zh: 素有消疾，自為宰相，謝病不視事，連年乞骸骨，帝每優寵之。

translations.literal: He had long suffered wasting-thirst illness; from the time he became chief minister he pleaded illness and did not attend to affairs, year after year begging for his bones, and the emperor each time treated him with exceptional favor.
translations.idiomatic: Plagued by chronic illness, he ceased to govern once he became chief minister, year after year asking to retire while the emperor showered him with indulgence.

sentences.zh: 令以公位歸第養疾，通復固辭。

translations.literal: He ordered him to return to his residence with ducal rank to nurse his illness; Tong again firmly declined.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told him to convalesce at home with his title; Li Tong still refused.

sentences.zh: 積二歲，乃聽上大司空印綬，以特進奉朝請。

translations.literal: After accumulating two years, he was permitted to offer up the great minister of works seals and ribbons and, as specially advanced, to attend court on summons.
translations.idiomatic: After two years he was allowed to surrender the great minister of works’ seal and keep only the “specially advanced” rank with occasional audience.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請封諸皇子，帝感通首創大謀，即日封通少子雄為召陵侯。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized requesting enfeoffment of the various imperial sons; the emperor, moved that Tong had been first to devise the great plan, that very day enfeoffed Tong’s youngest son Xiong as marquis of Shaoling.
translations.idiomatic: When ministers urged enfeoffing the princes, the emperor—mindful that Li Tong had first conceived the great design—on the spot made his youngest son Xiong marquis of Shaoling.

sentences.zh: 每幸南陽，常遣使者以太牢祠通父冢。

translations.literal: Whenever he visited Nanyang he always dispatched envoys with a grand sacrifice to offer at Tong’s father’s tomb.
translations.idiomatic: Each imperial visit to Nanyang brought a grand sacrifice to Li Shou’s grave.

sentences.zh: 十八年卒，謚曰恭侯。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year he died; his posthumous title was Marquis Gong.
translations.idiomatic: He died in year 18 with the posthumous epithet Gong (“the Reverent”).

sentences.zh: 帝及皇后親臨弔，送葬。

translations.literal: The emperor and empress personally attended mourning and saw off the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor and empress both walked in his funeral cortège.

sentences.zh: 子音嗣。

translations.literal: His son Yin succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yin inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 音卒，子定嗣。

translations.literal: When Yin died, his son Ding succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yin was followed by Ding.

sentences.zh: 定卒，子黃嗣。

translations.literal: When Ding died, his son Huang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Ding was followed by Huang.

sentences.zh: 黃卒，子壽嗣。

translations.literal: When Huang died, his son Shou succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Huang was followed by Shou.

sentences.zh: 李軼後為朱鮪所殺。

translations.literal: Li Yi afterward was killed by Zhu Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yi later fell to Zhu Wei’s blade.

sentences.zh: 更始之敗，李松戰死，唯通能以功名終。

translations.literal: At the defeat of Gengshi, Li Song died in battle; only Tong was able to end with merit and fame.
translations.idiomatic: When Gengshi fell, Li Song died fighting; of the Li clan only Li Tong finished life with honor.

sentences.zh: 永平中，顯宗幸宛，詔諸李隨安眾宗室會見，並受賞賜，恩寵篤焉。

translations.literal: In the Yongping period the Manifest Ancestor visited Wan and ordered the various Lis together with the imperial clan of Anzhong to have audience, and together they received rewards and gifts; favor and kindness were deep.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongping Emperor Ming summoned every branch of the Li family with the Anzhong imperial kin, heaped gifts on them, and showed uncommon favor.

sentences.zh: 論曰：子曰：『富與貴是人之所欲，不以其道得之，不處也。

translations.literal: The judgment says: The Master said, “Wealth and honor are what men desire; if they are not obtained by the right way, I will not dwell in them.”
translations.idiomatic: The historian comments: Confucius said wealth and rank are natural desires—yet they must be rightly won.

sentences.zh: 』李通豈知夫所欲而未識以道者乎！

translations.literal: ” Could Li Tong have known what men desire yet not recognized the way by which to obtain it!
translations.idiomatic: Did Li Tong grasp men’s cravings without seeing the path that justifies them?

sentences.zh: 夫天道性命，聖人難言之，況乃億測微隱，猖狂無妄之福，汙滅親宗，以觖一切之功哉！

translations.literal: As for the way of Heaven, nature, and destiny, even sages find it hard to speak; how much more to guess at hidden subtleties, wildly to trust unmerited good fortune, defile and destroy one’s kin and lineage, in hope of a moment’s success!
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s intent is hard enough for sages to read—how dare common men stake kindred lives on a gambler’s prophecy?

sentences.zh: 昔蒙穀負書，不徇楚難；

translations.literal: In olden times Meng Gu bore the documents on his back and did not follow Chu into disaster;
translations.idiomatic: Meng Gu fled Chu carrying the royal archives;

sentences.zh: 即墨用齊，義雪燕恥。

translations.literal: Jimo employed Qi’s power and in righteousness avenged Yan’s shame.
translations.idiomatic: the men of Jimo used Qi to wipe away Yan’s humiliation—

sentences.zh: 彼之趣捨所立，其殆與通異乎？

translations.literal: The choices they made in advancing or withdrawing—are they perhaps almost different from Tong’s?
translations.idiomatic: their sense of when to bend and when to stand was worlds apart from Li Tong’s choice.

sentences.zh: 王常字顏卿，潁川舞陽人也。

translations.literal: Wang Chang, styled Yanqing, was a man of Wuyang in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang of Wuyang in Yingchuan bore the style Yanqing.

sentences.zh: 王莽未，為弟報仇，亡命江夏。

translations.literal: Near the end of Wang Mang, to avenge his younger brother he killed a man and fled his life to Jiangxia.
translations.idiomatic: Under the last years of Wang Mang he avenged a brother by murder and fled to Jiangxia.

sentences.zh: 久之，與王鳳、王匡等起兵雲杜綠林中，聚眾數萬人，以常為偏裨，攻傍縣。

translations.literal: After a long time, together with Wang Feng, Wang Kuang, and others he raised troops amid the green woods of Yundu, gathering a multitude of several tens of thousands; they made Chang a lieutenant and attacked neighboring counties.
translations.idiomatic: Years later he joined Wang Feng and Wang Kuang in the Green Woods revolt at Yundu, commanding tens of thousands as a sub-general and raiding the nearby towns.

sentences.zh: 後與成丹、張卬別入南郡藍口，號下江兵。

translations.literal: Afterward together with Cheng Dan and Zhang Ang he separately entered Lankou in Nanjun and styled themselves the Lower Yang army.
translations.idiomatic: He later broke away with Cheng Dan and Zhang Ang to Lankou in Nanjun and took the name Lower Yang army.

sentences.zh: 王莽遣嚴尤、陳茂擊破之。

translations.literal: Wang Mang sent Yan You and Chen Mao to strike and defeat them.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang dispatched Yan You and Chen Mao and broke their host.

sentences.zh: 常與丹、卬收散卒入蔞谿，劫略鐘、龍間，眾復振。

translations.literal: Chang together with Dan and Ang gathered scattered troops, entered Louxi, plundered between Zhong and Long, and the multitude revived.
translations.idiomatic: Wang regrouped with Dan and Ang in Louxi, raided between Zhong and Long, and rebuilt his strength.

sentences.zh: 引軍與刑州牧戰於上唐，大破之，遂北至宜秋。

translations.literal: He led the army and fought the governor of Jing province at Shangtang, greatly defeated him, and thereupon went north to Yiqiu.
translations.idiomatic: He met the governor of Jingzhou at Shangtang, routed him, and marched north to Yiqiu.

sentences.zh: 是時，漢兵與新市、平林眾俱敗於小長安，各欲解去。

translations.literal: At this time the Han troops together with the Xinshi and Pinglin multitudes had both been defeated at Little Chang’an, and each wished to break up and leave.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the Han, Xinshi, and Pinglin columns had been shattered at Little Chang’an and were ready to scatter.

sentences.zh: 伯升聞下江軍在宜秋，即與光武及李通俱造常壁，曰：『願見下江一賢將，議大事。

translations.literal: Bosheng heard that the Lower Yang army was at Yiqiu, immediately together with Guangwu and Li Tong all went to Chang’s camp wall and said, “We beg to see one worthy general of the Lower Yang to deliberate a great affair.”
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng rode with Guangwu and Li Tong to Wang Chang’s camp and asked, “We would speak with one of your captains about a joint plan.”

sentences.zh: 』成丹、張卬共推遣常。

translations.literal: Cheng Dan and Zhang Ang jointly urged and sent Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng Dan and Zhang Ang sent Wang Chang to parley.

sentences.zh: 伯升見常，說以合從之利。

translations.literal: Bosheng saw Chang and expounded to him the profit of a united vertical alliance.
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng argued for a united front.

sentences.zh: 常大悟，曰：『王莽篡弒，殘虐天下，百姓思漢，故豪傑並起。

translations.literal: Chang greatly awakened and said, “Wang Mang usurped and murdered, cruelly oppressed the realm; the common people long for Han; therefore stalwarts rise together.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang saw the point: “Mang seized the throne by murder and tyrannized the empire; the people ache for Han—hence every bandit chief has risen.

sentences.zh: 今劉氏復興，即真主也。

translations.literal: Now the Liu clan has revived; that is the true lord.
translations.idiomatic: The Lius are stirring again—they are Heaven’s chosen house.

sentences.zh: 誠思出身為用，輔成大功。

translations.literal: I sincerely think to devote my person to service and assist in completing the great merit.”
translations.idiomatic: I mean to risk my life in their service and help finish the work.”

sentences.zh: 』伯升曰：『如事成，豈敢獨饗之哉！

translations.literal: Bosheng said, “If the affair succeeds, how dare I alone enjoy it!”
translations.idiomatic: Bosheng answered, “If we succeed, the glory will not be mine alone!”

sentences.zh: 』遂與常深相結而去。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon he became deeply bound with Chang and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They clasped agreement and parted.

sentences.zh: 常還，具為丹、卬言之。

translations.literal: Chang returned and fully reported it to Dan and Ang.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang laid the whole matter before Dan and Ang.

sentences.zh: 丹、卬負其從，皆曰：『大丈夫既起，當各自為主，何故受人制乎？

translations.literal: Dan and Ang, relying on their followers, all said, “Great men having already risen ought each to be his own master; why accept another’s control?”
translations.idiomatic: The two men, swollen with pride, snapped, “We raised this revolt—why take orders from anyone?”

sentences.zh: 』常心獨歸漢，乃稍曉說其將帥曰：『往者成、哀衰微無嗣，故王莽得承間篡位。

translations.literal: Chang’s heart alone turned to Han; thereupon he gradually enlightened his generals, saying, “In the past Cheng and Ai declined and had no heirs, therefore Wang Mang obtained the interval and usurped the throne.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang alone leaned toward Han and slowly persuaded his officers: “When Emperors Cheng and Ai failed, Mang slipped onto the throne.

sentences.zh: 既有天下，而政令苛酷，積失百姓之心。

translations.literal: Having already obtained the realm, his government orders were harsh and cruel, cumulatively losing the hearts of the common people.
translations.idiomatic: Once he ruled, his laws were cruel and he forfeited the people’s trust.

sentences.zh: 民之謳吟思漢，非一日也，故使吾屬因此得起。

translations.literal: The people’s songs and laments long for Han; it is not a single day; therefore he caused our sort thereby to be able to rise.
translations.idiomatic: Folk rhymes have begged for Han’s return for years—our armies rose from that hunger.

sentences.zh: 夫民所怨者，天所去也；

translations.literal: What the people resent, Heaven removes;
translations.idiomatic: What the people hate, Heaven casts off;

sentences.zh: 民所思者，天所與也。

translations.literal: what the people think on, Heaven grants.
translations.idiomatic: what they long for, Heaven grants.

sentences.zh: 舉大事，必當下順民心，上合天意，功乃可成。

translations.literal: In raising a great affair one must below comply with the people’s hearts and above match Heaven’s intent; only then may merit be completed.
translations.idiomatic: Any great venture must ride the people’s will and Heaven’s mind—only then can it succeed.

sentences.zh: 若負強恃勇，觸情恣欲，雖得天下，必復失之。

translations.literal: If one relies on strength and trusts in bravery, indulges feeling and gives free rein to desire, although one obtains the realm, one will certainly lose it again.
translations.idiomatic: Lean on brute courage, indulge every whim, and even if you seize the realm you will lose it again.

sentences.zh: 以秦、項之勢，尚至夷覆，況今布衣相聚草澤？

translations.literal: With the power of Qin and Xiang Yu they still reached utter destruction; how much more we who in plain cloth gather in the marshes and fens?
translations.idiomatic: Qin and Xiang Yu had the world in hand and still perished—what hope have ragtag marsh bandits?

sentences.zh: 以此行之，滅亡之道也。

translations.literal: To act by this is the road to extinction.
translations.idiomatic: That path leads only to ruin.”

sentences.zh: 今南陽諸劉舉宗起兵，觀其來議事者，皆有深計大慮，王公之才，與之並合，必成大功，此天所以祐吾屬也。

translations.literal: Now the various Liu of Nanyang have raised the lineage in arms; observing those who have come to deliberate, all have deep plans and great considerations, talent of king and duke; joining with them in merger, one will certainly complete a great merit; this is Heaven’s means of aiding our sort.”
translations.idiomatic: The Nanyang Lius who visit us are no rabble—they think like kings. Join them, and Heaven aids us all.”

sentences.zh: 』下江諸將雖屈強少識，然素敬常，乃皆謝曰：『無王將軍，吾屬幾陷於不義。

translations.literal: The Lower Yang generals, although stubborn and scant of insight, yet long had respected Chang; thereupon all apologized, saying, “Without General Wang we would almost have fallen into unrighteousness.
translations.idiomatic: The chiefs were rough men, yet they revered Wang Chang; they bowed their heads: “Without you, general, we had nearly chosen dishonor.

sentences.zh: 願敬受教。

translations.literal: We beg respectfully to receive instruction.”
translations.idiomatic: We hear you and obey.”

sentences.zh: 』即引兵與漢軍及新市、平林合。

translations.literal: ” Immediately they led troops and merged with the Han army and Xinshi and Pinglin.
translations.idiomatic: They marched at once to join the Han, Xinshi, and Pinglin hosts.

sentences.zh: 於是諸部齊心同力，銳氣益壯，遂俱進，破殺甄阜、梁丘賜。

translations.literal: Thereupon the various divisions were of one heart and united strength; keen spirit grew ever stronger, and thereupon they jointly advanced, broke, and killed Zhen Fu and Liangqiu Ci.
translations.idiomatic: United, their ardor surged; together they fell on Zhen Fu and Liangqiu Ci and destroyed them.

sentences.zh: 及諸將議立宗室，唯常與南陽士大夫同意欲立伯升，而朱鮪、張卬等不聽。

translations.literal: When the generals deliberated on establishing a member of the imperial clan, only Chang together with the Nanyang scholar-officials agreed in wishing to establish Bosheng, but Zhu Wei, Zhang Ang, and others would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: When the chiefs debated which Liu to crown, Wang Chang and the Nanyang gentry backed Bosheng; Zhu Wei and Zhang Ang overruled them.

sentences.zh: 及更始立，以常為廷尉、大將軍，封知命侯。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, he appointed Chang minister of justice and great general and enfeoffed him as marquis who knows destiny.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi named him minister of justice and great general, with the title marquis who knows destiny.

sentences.zh: 別徇汝南、沛郡，還入昆陽，與光武共擊破王尋、王邑。

translations.literal: Separately he took Runan and Pei commandery, returned and entered Kunyang, and together with Guangwu jointly struck and defeated Wang Xun and Wang Yi.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned through Runan and Pei, re-entered Kunyang, and with Guangwu broke Wang Xun and Wang Yi.

sentences.zh: 更始西都長安，以常行南陽太守事，令專命誅賞，封為鄧王，食八縣，賜姓劉氏。

translations.literal: Gengshi moved the capital west to Chang’an; he appointed Chang acting governor of Nanyang, ordered him sole authority over executions and rewards, enfeoffed him as king of Deng, with food from eight counties, and granted the surname Liu.
translations.idiomatic: At the western capital he made Wang Chang acting governor of Nanyang with full penal and reward powers, enfeoffed him as king of Deng over eight counties, and bestowed the imperial surname Liu.

sentences.zh: 常性恭儉，遵法度，南方稱之。

translations.literal: Chang by nature was respectful and frugal, observed the statutes, and the south praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Modest and law-abiding, he won praise throughout the south.

sentences.zh: 後帝於大會中指常謂群臣曰：『此家率下江諸將輔翼漢室，心如金石，真忠臣也。

translations.literal: Afterward at a great assembly the emperor pointed to Chang and said to the host of ministers, “This man led the Lower Yang generals in supporting the Han house; his heart is like metal and stone—a true loyal minister.”
translations.idiomatic: Later Guangwu told his court, “This man brought the Lower Yang host to Han’s side; his loyalty is cast in bronze and stone.”

sentences.zh: 』是日遷常為漢忠將軍，遣南擊鄧奉、董訢，令諸將皆屬焉。

translations.literal: ” That day he promoted Chang to Han loyal general and sent him south to strike Deng Feng and Dong Xin, ordering the various generals all to be subordinate to him.
translations.idiomatic: The same day he named him Han loyal general and sent him south against Deng Feng and Dong Xin with every general under his command.

sentences.zh: 又詔常北擊河間、漁陽，平諸屯聚。

translations.literal: He also ordered Chang north to strike Hejian and Yuyang and pacify the various encampments and gatherings.
translations.idiomatic: He also sent him north to pacify Hejian and Yuyang.

sentences.zh: 五年秋，攻拔湖陵，又與帝會任城，因從破蘇茂、龐萌。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, autumn, he attacked and took Huling, again met the emperor at Rencheng, and thereupon followed in defeating Su Mao and Pang Meng.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth autumn he took Huling, joined the emperor at Rencheng, and helped crush Su Mao and Pang Meng.

sentences.zh: 進攻下邳，常部當城門戰，一日數合，賊反走入城，常追迫之，城上射矢雨下，帝從百餘騎自城南高處望，常戰力甚，馳遣中黃門詔使引還，賊遂降。

translations.literal: Advancing to attack Xiapi, Chang’s division fought at the city gate battle, several engagements in one day; the bandits turned and ran into the city; Chang pressed them in pursuit; on the city arrows rained down; the emperor with more than a hundred riders watched from a height south of the city; Chang fought with extreme force; he galloped and sent a palace eunuch with an edict ordering him to lead back, and the bandits thereupon surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: At Xiapi Wang Chang’s men stormed the gate again and again until the rebels broke; he chased them under a storm of arrows while Guangwu watched from a southern knoll with a hundred horsemen. Seeing the fury of the fight, the emperor sent a eunuch with orders to break off—and the enemy then yielded.

sentences.zh: 又別率騎都尉王霸共平沛郡賊。

translations.literal: Moreover separately he led the cavalry commandant Wang Ba together to pacify the bandits of Pei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He also joined cavalry commandant Wang Ba to clear Pei of bandits.

sentences.zh: 六年春，征還洛陽，令夫人迎常於舞陽，歸家上冢。

translations.literal: In the sixth year, spring, he was summoned back to Luoyang; he ordered the empress to welcome Chang at Wuyang and return home to ascend the tombs.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth spring he was recalled to Luoyang; the emperor sent the empress to greet him at Wuyang and accompany him home to sweep his ancestors’ graves.

sentences.zh: 西屯長安，拒隗囂。

translations.literal: He camped west at Chang’an to resist Wei Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: He took up a western post at Chang’an to hold Wei Xiao at bay.

sentences.zh: 七年，使使者持璽書，即拜常為橫野大將軍，位次與諸將絕席。

translations.literal: In the seventh year he sent an envoy bearing the imperial jade document and on the spot appointed Chang general who sweeps the wilderness; his seat order was set apart from the other generals by a separate mat.
translations.idiomatic: In year 7 an imperial messenger invested him as general who sweeps the wilderness, ranking above the other field commanders.

sentences.zh: 常別擊破隗囂將高峻於朝那。

translations.literal: Chang separately struck and defeated Wei Xiao's general Gao Jun at Chaona.
translations.idiomatic: He smashed Gao Jun, one of Wei Xiao’s generals, at Chaona.

sentences.zh: 囂遣將過烏氏，常要擊破之。

translations.literal: Xiao sent a general across Wushi; Chang intercepted and defeated him.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiao tried to push a column past Wushi, Wang ambushed and broke it.

sentences.zh: 轉降保塞羌諸營壁，皆平之。

translations.literal: He turned and subdued the various stockades of the Qiang who guarded the frontier, pacifying them all.
translations.idiomatic: He next pacified every stockaded camp of the garrison Qiang along the border.

sentences.zh: 九年，擊內黃賊，破降之。

translations.literal: In the ninth year he struck the bandits of Neihuang, defeated them, and brought them to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: In year 9 he crushed the Neihuang rebels and accepted their surrender.

sentences.zh: 後北屯故安，拒盧芳。

translations.literal: Afterward he camped north at Gu’an to resist Lu Fang.
translations.idiomatic: He then moved north to Gu’an to block Lu Fang.

sentences.zh: 十二年，薨於屯所，謚曰節侯。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year he died at his camp post; his posthumous title was Marquis Jie.
translations.idiomatic: He died in camp in year 12 with the posthumous name Jie (“the Constant”).

sentences.zh: 子廣嗣。

translations.literal: His son Guang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Guang inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 三十年，徙封石城侯。

translations.literal: In the thirtieth year he was moved and enfeoffed as marquis of Shicheng.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 30 the fief was transferred to Shicheng.

sentences.zh: 永平十四年，坐與楚事相連，國除。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year of Yongping he sat guilty of connection with the Chu affair, and the kingdom was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 14 he lost the title for ties to the Chu conspiracy.

sentences.zh: 鄧晨字偉卿，南陽新野人也。

translations.literal: Deng Chen, styled Weiqing, was a man of Xinye in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Chen of Xinye in Nanyang bore the style Weiqing.

sentences.zh: 世吏二千石。

translations.literal: For generations they had served as two-thousand-bushel officials.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears had held ministerial rank for generations.

sentences.zh: 父宏，預章都尉。

translations.literal: His father Hong was commandant of Yuzhang.
translations.idiomatic: His father Hong had been commandant of Yuzhang.

sentences.zh: 晨初聚光武姊元。

translations.literal: In the beginning Chen married Guangwu’s elder sister Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: He had married Guangwu’s sister Liu Yuan in his youth.

sentences.zh: 王莽末，光武嘗與兄伯升及晨俱之宛，與穰人蔡少公等宴語。

translations.literal: Near the end of Wang Mang, Guangwu once together with his elder brother Bosheng and Chen all went to Wan and feasted and conversed with Cai Shaogong of Rang and others.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Wang Mang’s reign Guangwu, Bosheng, and Deng Chen once visited Wan and dined with Cai Shaogong of Rang and other local men.

sentences.zh: 少公頗學圖讖，言劉秀當為天子。

translations.literal: Shaogong had studied charts and prophecies somewhat and said that Liu Xiu ought to become Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Shaogong dabbled in prophecy and declared that a man named Liu Xiu would mount the throne.

sentences.zh: 或曰：『是國師公劉秀乎？

translations.literal: Someone said, “Is it the State Teacher’s Liu Xiu?”
translations.idiomatic: “You mean the court astrologer Liu Xiu?” someone asked.

sentences.zh: 』光武戲曰：『何用知非仆耶？

translations.literal: Guangwu jested and said, “How do you know it is not this humble fellow?”
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu laughed. “Who says it isn’t your humble host?”

sentences.zh: 』坐者皆大笑，晨心獨喜。

translations.literal: Those seated all laughed greatly; Chen’s heart alone rejoiced.
translations.idiomatic: The table roared with laughter—only Deng Chen’s pulse leapt.

sentences.zh: 及光武與家屬避吏新野，舍晨廬，甚相親愛。

translations.literal: When Guangwu together with family dependents was avoiding clerical pursuit at Xinye, he lodged at Chen’s hut and was mutually intimate and dear.
translations.idiomatic: When Guangwu hid from the warrant at Xinye, he bunked with Deng Chen and grew closer than ever.

sentences.zh: 晨因謂光武曰：『王莽悖暴，盛夏斬人，此天亡之時也。

translations.literal: Chen thereupon said to Guangwu, “Wang Mang is perverse and cruel; in the height of summer he executes men—this is the moment when Heaven is destroying him.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Chen urged him, “Mang slaughters men even in midsummer—Heaven is done with him.

sentences.zh: 往時會宛，獨當應耶？

translations.literal: When we met at Wan before, was it only another man who was meant to answer the omen?”
translations.idiomatic: That prophecy at Wan—surely it pointed to you?”

sentences.zh: 』光武笑不答。

translations.literal: Guangwu laughed and did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu smiled and said nothing.

sentences.zh: 及漢兵起，晨將賓客會棘陽。

translations.literal: When the Han troops rose, Chen led retainers and met them at Jiyang.
translations.idiomatic: When revolt flared, Deng Chen brought his clients to Jiyang.

sentences.zh: 漢兵敗小長安，諸將多亡家屬，光武單馬遁走。

translations.literal: The Han troops were defeated at Little Chang’an; many of the generals lost family dependents; Guangwu on a single horse fled and ran.
translations.idiomatic: After the rout at Little Chang’an most officers lost kin; Guangwu bolted alone on one horse.

sentences.zh: 遇女弟伯姬，與共騎而奔。

translations.literal: He met his younger sister Boji and together with her rode double and fled.
translations.idiomatic: He met his sister Boji and doubled her on the saddle to escape.

sentences.zh: 前行復見元，超令上馬。

translations.literal: Going forward ahead he again saw Yuan and urgently ordered her to mount the horse.
translations.idiomatic: Farther on he found Yuan and shouted for her to climb up behind him.

sentences.zh: 元以手撝曰：『行矣，不能相救，無為兩沒也。

translations.literal: Yuan with her hand waved them off, saying, “Go on; you cannot save us both—do not let us perish together for nothing.”
translations.idiomatic: Yuan waved them on: “Run—do not drown us all in one grave.”

sentences.zh: 』會追兵至，元及三女皆遇害。

translations.literal: It happened that pursuing troops arrived; Yuan together with three daughters were all killed in the fighting.
translations.idiomatic: The pursuers caught her; Yuan and her three daughters fell together.

sentences.zh: 漢兵退保棘陽，而新野宰乃汙晨宅，焚其冢墓。

translations.literal: The Han troops withdrew to hold Jiyang, but the magistrate of Xinye thereupon defiled Chen’s house and burned his tombs.
translations.idiomatic: The Han line fell back to Jiyang while Xinye’s magistrate sacked Deng Chen’s home and torched his family graves.

sentences.zh: 宗族皆恚怒，曰：『家自富足，何故隨婦家人入湯鑊中？

translations.literal: The lineage group all were indignant and angry and said, “Our house is itself wealthy and sufficient; why follow wife’s kin into the boiling cauldron?”
translations.idiomatic: His clan raged: “We were rich enough—why follow in-laws into the cauldron?”

sentences.zh: 』最終無恨色。

translations.literal: Chen to the end had no resentful expression.
translations.idiomatic: Through it all Deng Chen never showed a trace of bitterness.

sentences.zh: 更始立，以晨為偏將軍。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, he appointed Chen lieutenant general.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi named him lieutenant general.

sentences.zh: 與光武略地潁川，俱夜出昆陽城，擊破王尋、王邑。

translations.literal: Together with Guangwu he took territory in Yingchuan; both went out of Kunyang city by night and struck and defeated Wang Xun and Wang Yi.
translations.idiomatic: He joined Guangwu in a night sortie from Kunyang that broke Wang Xun and Wang Yi.

sentences.zh: 又別徇陽翟以東，至京、密，皆下之。

translations.literal: Moreover separately he took the region east of Yangdi as far as Jing and Mi, all subduing them.
translations.idiomatic: He then swept east from Yangdi through Jing and Mi, taking every town.

sentences.zh: 更始北都洛陽，以晨為常山太守。

translations.literal: Gengshi moved the capital north to Luoyang and appointed Chen governor of Changshan.
translations.idiomatic: When Gengshi moved the court to Luoyang, Deng Chen became governor of Changshan.

sentences.zh: 會王郎反，光武自薊走信都，晨亦間行會於巨鹿下，自請從擊邯鄲。

translations.literal: It happened that Wang Lang rebelled; Guangwu fled from Ji to Xinyang; Chen also by stealthy routes met him below Julu and personally requested to follow in striking Handan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Lang’s revolt drove Guangwu from Ji to Xinyang; Deng Chen stole through enemy lines to Julu and begged to march on Handan at his side.

sentences.zh: 光武曰：『偉卿以一身從我，不如以一郡為我北道主人。

translations.literal: Guangwu said, “Weiqing, in following me with a single person, is not as good as holding one commandery as my host on the northern road.”
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu replied, “Better you feed my army from a whole commandery than ride at my elbow.”

sentences.zh: 』乃遣晨歸郡。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon he sent Chen back to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He sent him back to govern Changshan.

sentences.zh: 光武追銅馬、高胡群賊於冀州，晨發積射士千人，又遣委輸給軍不絕。

translations.literal: Guangwu pursued the Bronze Horse and Gao Hu bandit hosts in Jizhou; Chen raised a thousand crossbowmen of the accumulated-shooting corps and moreover sent relay supplies to the army without cease.
translations.idiomatic: While Guangwu chased the Bronze Horse through Ji, Deng Chen levied a thousand crossbowmen and kept supply wagons rolling.

sentences.zh: 光武即位，封晨房子侯。

translations.literal: When Guangwu ascended the throne, he enfeoffed Chen as marquis of Fangzi.
translations.idiomatic: At his accession Guangwu made him marquis of Fangzi.

sentences.zh: 帝又感悼姊沒於亂兵，追封謚元為新野節義長公主，立廟於縣西。

translations.literal: The emperor moreover grieved that his elder sister had perished amid mutinous troops; he posthumously enfeoffed and gave Yuan the title senior princess of Xinye, chaste and righteous, and established a temple west of the county.
translations.idiomatic: He posthumously honored sister Yuan as senior princess of Xinye, paragon of fidelity, and built her a shrine west of the town.

sentences.zh: 封晨長子汎為吳房侯，以奉公主之祀。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Chen’s eldest son Fan as marquis of Wufang to attend the princess’s sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: His eldest son Fan received Wufang to maintain her cult.

sentences.zh: 建武三年，征晨還京師，數宴見，說故舊平生為歡。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianwu he summoned Chen back to the capital, repeatedly feasted and received him in audience, discoursing on old friends and their whole lives as pleasure.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 3 he recalled Deng Chen to the capital for long banquets and talk of old times.

sentences.zh: 晨從容謂帝曰：『仆竟辦之。

translations.literal: Chen calmly said to the emperor, “Your servant has indeed brought it off.”
translations.idiomatic: Deng Chen murmured, “So I was right after all.”

sentences.zh: 』帝大笑。

translations.literal: The emperor laughed greatly.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor roared with laughter.

sentences.zh: 從幸章陵，拜光祿大夫，使持節監執金吾賈復等擊平邵陵、新息賊。

translations.literal: Following in attendance to Zhangling, he was appointed superintendent of the imperial household, sent bearing credentials to supervise chief commandant of the guards Jia Fu and others in striking and pacifying the bandits of Shaoling and Xinyang.
translations.idiomatic: At Zhangling he became superintendent of the imperial household and, staff in hand, oversaw Jia Fu’s campaign against Shaoling and Xinyang bandits.

sentences.zh: 四年，從幸壽春，留鎮九江。

translations.literal: In the fourth year he followed in attendance to Shouchun and remained to garrison Jiujiang.
translations.idiomatic: In year 4 he accompanied the tour to Shouchun and stayed to hold Jiujiang.

sentences.zh: 晨好樂郡職，由是復拜為中山太守，吏民稱之，常為冀州高第。

translations.literal: Chen loved the joy of commandery duty; for this reason he was again appointed governor of Zhongshan; clerks and commoners praised him, and he was always top rank in Ji province.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Chen loved life as a local governor; Zhongshan’s people adored him, and his ratings topped Ji province year after year.

sentences.zh: 十三年，更封南䜌侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year he was again enfeoffed as marquis of Nanxiang.
translations.idiomatic: In year 13 his title was moved to Nanxiang.

sentences.zh: 入奉朝請，復為汝南太守。

translations.literal: He entered to attend court on summons and again became governor of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled to court, he soon returned as governor of Runan.

sentences.zh: 十八年，行幸章陵，征晨行廷尉事。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year on progress to Zhangling he summoned Chen to act as minister of justice.
translations.idiomatic: On the Zhangling tour of year 18 he named Deng Chen acting minister of justice.

sentences.zh: 從至新野，置酒酣宴，賞賜數百千萬，復遣歸郡。

translations.literal: Following to Xinye, he set wine for a merry feast, bestowed rewards of several hundred thousand to a million, again sent him back to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: At Xinye the emperor threw a feast and poured out gold by the million before sending him back south.

sentences.zh: 晨興鴻郤陂數千頃田，汝土以殷，魚稻之饒，流衍它郡。

translations.literal: Chen raised the fields of the Hongxi reservoir to several thousand qing; the soil of Runan thereby became rich, and the abundance of fish and rice overflowed into other commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: He diked the Hongxi marsh into thousands of qing of paddy until Runan’s grain and fish fed neighboring commanderies.

sentences.zh: 明年，定封西華侯，復征奉朝請。

translations.literal: The next year he fixed the enfeoffment as marquis of Xihua and again was summoned to attend court on summons.
translations.idiomatic: The next year his fief was fixed at Xihua, then he was recalled once more on summons.

sentences.zh: 二十五年卒，詔遣中謁者備公主官屬禮儀，招迎新野主魂，與晨合葬於北芒。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fifth year he died; an edict ordered the palace usher to prepare the princess’s household ritual and receive the soul of the senior princess of Xinye, burying her together with Chen on Mang Hill north of Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: He died in year 25; the court staged a princess-level rite, summoned Lady Yuan’s spirit from Xinye, and laid husband and wife together on the Mang plateau.

sentences.zh: 乘輿與中宮親臨喪送葬。

translations.literal: The imperial carriage and inner palace personally attended mourning and saw off the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor and empress walked behind his bier.

sentences.zh: 謚曰惠侯。

translations.literal: His posthumous title was Marquis Hui.
translations.idiomatic: His posthumous name was Hui (“the Kind”).

sentences.zh: 小子棠嗣，後徙封武當。

translations.literal: His younger son Tang succeeded; afterward he was moved and enfeoffed at Wudang.
translations.idiomatic: His younger son Tang inherited, later removed to Wudang.

sentences.zh: 棠卒，子固嗣。

translations.literal: When Tang died, his son Gu succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Tang was followed by Gu.

sentences.zh: 固卒，子國嗣。

translations.literal: When Gu died, his son Guo succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Gu was followed by Guo.

sentences.zh: 國卒，子福嗣，永建元年卒，無子，國除。

translations.literal: When Guo died, his son Fu succeeded; in the first year of Yongjian he died without a son, and the kingdom was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Guo’s line ended with Fu, who died childless in Yongjian 1; the fief lapsed.

sentences.zh: 來歙字君叔，南陽新野人也。

translations.literal: Lai Xi, styled Junshu, was a man of Xinye in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi of Xinye in Nanyang took the style Junshu.

sentences.zh: 六世祖漢，有才力，武帝世，以光祿大夫副樓船將軍楊仆，擊破南越、朝鮮。

translations.literal: His sixth-generation ancestor Han had talent and strength; in Emperor Wu’s era, as grand counselor of the palace assisting tower-ship general Yang Pu, he struck and defeated Southern Yue and Joseon.
translations.idiomatic: Six generations back, Lai Han had served Emperor Wu as deputy to tower-ship general Yang Pu in the conquests of Nanyue and Korea.

sentences.zh: 父仲，哀帝時為諫大夫，娶光武祖姑，生歙。

translations.literal: His father Zhong in Emperor Ai’s time was grandee remonstrant; he married Guangwu’s great-aunt on the father’s side and bore Xi.
translations.idiomatic: His father Zhong, a remonstrant under Emperor Ai, had married one of Guangwu’s great-aunts and fathered Xi.

sentences.zh: 光武甚親敬之，數共往來長安。

translations.literal: Guangwu was very intimate with and respected him, repeatedly going back and forth to Chang’an together.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu loved him like an elder brother and often traveled with him to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 漢兵起，王莽以歙劉氏外屬，乃收系之，賓客共篡奪，得免。

translations.literal: When the Han troops rose, Wang Mang, because Xi was an external relative of the Liu clan, thereupon seized and bound him; retainers together snatched him away in a raid, and he obtained release.
translations.idiomatic: When revolt began, Wang Mang jailed him as Liu kin; his clients broke him out.

sentences.zh: 更始即位，以歙為吏，從入關。

translations.literal: When Gengshi ascended the throne, he appointed Xi as an official and followed in entering the Pass.
translations.idiomatic: Gengshi gave him office and took him west.

sentences.zh: 數言事不用，以病去。

translations.literal: Several times he spoke on affairs and was not employed; citing illness he departed.
translations.idiomatic: His counsel went unheard, so he quit on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 歙女弟為漢中王劉嘉妻，嘉遣人迎歙，因南之漢中。

translations.literal: Xi’s younger sister was wife to Liu Jia, king of Hanzhong; Jia sent men to welcome Xi, and thereby he went south to Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: His sister was wife to Liu Jia of Hanzhong, who welcomed him south.

sentences.zh: 更始敗，歙勸嘉歸光武，遂與嘉俱東詣洛陽。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was defeated, Xi urged Jia to submit to Guangwu; thereupon together with Jia they went east to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: After Gengshi fell he persuaded Jia to yield to Guangwu, and both men rode east to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 帝見歙，大歡，即解衣為衣之，拜為太中大夫。

translations.literal: The emperor, seeing Xi, was greatly pleased, immediately took off his robe and clothed him with it, and appointed him grand counselor of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor stripped off his own cloak to wrap him in and named him grand counselor of the palace.

sentences.zh: 是時方以隴、蜀為憂，獨謂歙曰：『今西州未附，子陽稱帝，道里阻遠，諸將方務關東，思西州方略，未知所任，其謀若何？

translations.literal: At the time he was just taking Long and Shu as his worry; alone he said to Xi, “Now the western provinces are not yet attached; Ziyang has declared himself emperor; the roads are blocked and far; the generals are all busy east of the passes; pondering strategy for the western provinces, I do not yet know whom to entrust—what is your plan?”
translations.idiomatic: With Long and Shu still outside the fold, he asked Xi alone, “Gongsun Shu reigns in the west, the roads are long, and every general is tied down in the east—who can take that theater?”

sentences.zh: 』歙因自請曰：『臣嘗與隗囂相遇長安。

translations.literal: Xi thereupon personally requested, saying, “Your servant once met Wei Xiao at Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Xi volunteered: “I knew Wei Xiao in Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 其入始起，以漢為名。

translations.literal: When he first rose, he took Han as his name.
translations.idiomatic: He first raised arms in Han’s name.

sentences.zh: 今陛下聖德隆興，臣願得奉威命，開以丹青之信，囂必束手自歸，則述自亡之勢，不足圖也。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty’s sagely virtue flourishes and rises; your servant wishes to obtain to carry awesome commands and open him with the good faith of cinnabar and green; Xiao is sure to bind his own hands and return, and then Shu’s situation of self-destruction will follow—there is no need to scheme it.”
translations.idiomatic: Let me go with your writ: I can win Xiao with plain good faith; once he yields, Gongsun Shu collapses of himself.”

sentences.zh: 』帝然之。

translations.literal: The emperor approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 建武三年，歙始使隗囂。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianwu Xi first went as envoy to Wei Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 3 he made his first mission to Wei Xiao.

sentences.zh: 五年，復持節送馬援，因奉璽書於囂。

translations.literal: In the fifth year again bearing the staff he escorted Ma Yuan and thereupon presented the imperial jade document to Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: In year 5 he escorted Ma Yuan with staff and jade edict to Longxi.

sentences.zh: 既還，復往說囂。

translations.literal: Having returned, he again went to persuade Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Back in the capital, he returned to argue with Xiao.

sentences.zh: 囂遂遣子恂隨歙入質，拜歙為中郎將。

translations.literal: Xiao thereupon sent his son Xun to follow Xi into hostage service at the capital, and Xi was appointed zhonglang general (gentleman of the palace).
translations.idiomatic: Xiao sent his heir Xun to Luoyang with Xi as hostage; the court then named Lai Xi a zhonglang general.

sentences.zh: 時山東略定，帝謀西收囂兵，與俱伐蜀，復使歙喻旨囂將王元說囂，多設疑，故久豫不決。

translations.literal: At the time east of the mountains was largely pacified; the emperor planned westward to gather Xiao’s troops and together with them attack Shu; he again sent Xi to convey his intent; Xiao’s general Wang Yuan persuaded Xiao, setting up many doubts, and therefore for long he hesitated and could not decide.
translations.idiomatic: The plain was nearly won; Guangwu meant to draft Xiao’s army for an attack on Shu and sent Xi again—but Wang Yuan filled Xiao’s ear with doubts, and month after month Xiao would not move.

sentences.zh: 歙素剛毅，遂發憤質責囂曰：『國家以君知臧否，曉廢興，故以手書暢意。

translations.literal: Xi by nature was firm and severe; thereupon in anger he confronted and rebuked Xiao, saying, “The state, because you know what is approved and what is rejected, understands rise and fall, therefore with a personal letter expresses its intent.
translations.idiomatic: Hot-tempered, Lai Xi rounded on Xiao: “The court trusts you to know right from ruin—hence the emperor’s own letter.

sentences.zh: 足下推忠誠，遣伯春委質，是臣主之交信也。

translations.literal: You, sir, have pushed loyalty and good faith, sent Bochun to give hostage service—that is the mutual trust between minister and lord.
translations.idiomatic: You pledged faith and sent your son as hostage—that bound lord and vassal.

sentences.zh: 今反欲用佞惑之言，為族滅之計，叛主負子，違背忠信乎？

translations.literal: Now you instead wish to use flattering, delusive words and pursue a plan that exterminates the lineage; do you rebel against your lord and betray your son, turning your back on loyalty and good faith?
translations.idiomatic: Now you toy with slander and risk your whole clan—would you disown your sovereign and your child?”

sentences.zh: 吉凶之決，在於今日。

translations.literal: The decision between fortune and disaster lies in this day.
translations.idiomatic: Choose today—life or extinction.”

sentences.zh: 』欲前刺囂，囂起入，部勒兵，將殺歙，歙徐杖節就車而去。

translations.literal: ” He wished to step forward and stab Xiao; Xiao rose and entered, arrayed troops, and was about to kill Xi; Xi calmly leaned on his staff, went to his cart, and departed.
translations.idiomatic: He lunged to stab Xiao; Xiao fled within, called out guards, and would have killed him—yet Xi leaned on his credentials, walked to his carriage, and drove off.

sentences.zh: 囂愈怒，王元勸囂殺歙，使牛邯將兵圍守之。

translations.literal: Xiao grew angrier; Wang Yuan urged Xiao to kill Xi and sent Niu Han to lead troops to surround and guard him.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao’s rage flared; Wang Yuan urged execution and sent Niu Han to pen him in.

sentences.zh: 囂將王遵諫曰：『愚聞為國者慎器與名，為家者畏怨重禍。

translations.literal: Xiao’s general Wang Zun remonstrated, saying, “Your foolish servant has heard that one who acts for the state is careful with vessels and names, and one who acts for the family fears resentment and heavy disaster.
translations.idiomatic: General Wang Zun warned him: “A state guards its ritual emblems; a household dreads needless blood-feud.

sentences.zh: 俱慎名器，則下服其命；

translations.literal: If both are careful with names and vessels, then subordinates will submit to their commands;
translations.idiomatic: Honor both, and men obey;

sentences.zh: 輕用怨禍，則家受其殃。

translations.literal: if one lightly employs resentment and disaster, then the family receives its calamity.
translations.idiomatic: trifle with feud, and your house burns.”

sentences.zh: 今將軍遣子質漢，內懷它誌，名器逆矣；

translations.literal: Now the general has sent his son as hostage to Han while harboring other intent in the heart—names and vessels are reversed;
translations.idiomatic: You sent a hostage yet plot betrayal—that inverts the rite of rank.

sentences.zh: 外人有議欲謀漢使，輕怨禍矣。

translations.literal: outsiders have proposals wishing to plot against the Han envoy—this is lightly to invite resentment and disaster.
translations.idiomatic: To murder an imperial envoy is to court that feud.

sentences.zh: 古者列國兵交，使在其間，所以重兵貴和而不任戰也，何況承王命籍重質而犯之哉？

translations.literal: In olden times when feudal states crossed arms, envoys remained between them—thereby they valued arms and honored harmony and did not trust to battle; how much more when one bears the king’s command and relies on a weighty hostage yet violates it?
translations.idiomatic: Ancient states at war still spared envoys—how then strike a man who bears the Son of Heaven’s writ and your son’s life?”

sentences.zh: 君叔雖單車遠使，而陛下之外兄也。

translations.literal: Although Junshu came alone on a distant mission, he is nonetheless Your Majesty’s cousin on the mother’s side.
translations.idiomatic: Junshu rides alone, yet he is the emperor’s cousin.

sentences.zh: 害之無損於漢，而隨以族滅。

translations.literal: To harm him does no harm to Han, yet with it follows extermination of the lineage.
translations.idiomatic: Killing him gains nothing for Han but costs you your clan.

sentences.zh: 昔宋執楚使，遂有析骸易子之禍。

translations.literal: In olden times Song seized the Chu envoy, and thereafter there was the disaster of splitting bones and exchanging children.
translations.idiomatic: Song once seized a Chu envoy and brought on the famine where men boiled bones and swapped children.

sentences.zh: 小國猶不可辱，況於萬乘之主，重以伯春之命哉！

translations.literal: Small states still may not be insulted; how much more the lord of ten thousand chariots, with redoubled weight of Bochun’s life!
translations.idiomatic: Petty kingdoms could not endure such shame—what of a Son of Heaven who holds your heir?”

sentences.zh: 』歙為人有信義，言行不違，及往來遊說，皆可案復，西州士大夫皆信重之，多為其言，故得免而東歸。

translations.literal: Xi as a man had good faith and righteousness; his words and deeds did not contradict; when he went back and forth persuading, all could be checked and verified; scholar-officials of the western provinces all trusted and esteemed him, and many spoke for him; therefore he obtained release and returned east.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi’s word had always been bond; every pledge he made in the west could be verified, and the gentry of Long spoke up until Xiao let him ride east alive.

sentences.zh: 八年春，歙與征虜將軍祭遵襲略陽，遵道病還，分遣精兵隨歙，合二千餘人，伐山開道，從番須、回中徑至略陽，斬囂守將金梁，因保其城。

translations.literal: In the spring of the eighth year Xi together with the general who captures bandits Ji Zun raided Lueyang; Zun took sick on the road and returned, separately dispatching picked troops to follow Xi, totaling more than two thousand men; they cut mountains and opened roads, by the route through Fanshu and Huizhong went straight to Lueyang, beheaded Xiao’s defending general Jin Liang, and thereupon held the city.
translations.idiomatic: In spring of year 8 Lai Xi and Ji Zun struck Lueyang; when Ji Zun fell ill, Xi took two thousand picked men, hacked a path through Fanshu and Huizhong, slew Jin Liang, and seized the town.

sentences.zh: 囂大驚曰：『何其神也！

translations.literal: Xiao greatly startled said, “How can they be so spirit-like!”
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiao gasped, “How did they reach here like ghosts?”

sentences.zh: 』乃悉兵數萬人圍略陽，斬山築堤，激水灌城。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon with all his troops, several tens of thousands, he besieged Lueyang, cut the mountain to build dikes, and dashed water to flood the city.
translations.idiomatic: He then threw tens of thousands against the walls, dammed streams, and tried to drown the garrison.

sentences.zh: 歙與將士固死堅守，矢盡，乃發屋斷木以為兵。

translations.literal: Xi together with officers and soldiers firmly defended to the death; when arrows were exhausted, they tore roofs and broke timber to make weapons.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi and his men held out until every arrow was gone, then tore beams from houses to fight on.

sentences.zh: 囂盡銳攻之，自春至秋，其士卒疲弊，帝乃大發關東兵，自將上隴，囂眾潰走，圍解。

translations.literal: Xiao attacked with all his picked troops from spring to autumn; his soldiers and troops were weary and exhausted; the emperor thereupon greatly issued troops east of the passes, personally led them up Long, Xiao’s multitude broke and fled, and the siege was lifted.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiao hammered the walls from spring to fall until his army wilted; Guangwu then drove up Long with fresh hosts from the east, Xiao’s lines collapsed, and the siege broke.

sentences.zh: 於是置酒高會，勞賜歙，班坐絕席，在諸將之右，賜歙妻縑千匹。

translations.literal: Thereupon he set wine for a high assembly, rewarded and feasted Xi, arranged seats with a separate mat, to the right of the various generals, and bestowed on Xi’s wife one thousand bolts of plain silk.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor feasted the army, seated Lai Xi above the other generals on his own mat, and gave his wife a thousand rolls of silk.

sentences.zh: 詔使留屯長安，悉監護諸將。

translations.literal: An edict ordered him to remain garrisoned at Chang’an and fully supervise and protect the various generals.
translations.idiomatic: He was ordered to stay in Chang’an as overseer of every western column.

sentences.zh: 歙因上書曰：『公孫述以隴西、天水為藩蔽，故得延命假息。

translations.literal: Xi thereupon submitted a memorial saying, “Gongsun Shu takes Longxi and Tianshui as screen and shield; therefore he is able to prolong his life and borrow breath.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi memorialized: “Shu survives only while Longxi and Tianshui shield him.

sentences.zh: 今二郡平蕩，則述智計窮矣。

translations.literal: Now that the two commanderies are leveled and swept, then Shu’s stratagems are exhausted.”
translations.idiomatic: Crush those two commanderies and his schemes are spent.”

sentences.zh: 宜益選兵馬，儲積資糧。

translations.literal: It is fitting further to select troops and horses and store up supplies and grain.
translations.idiomatic: Hoard grain and remount the army.”

sentences.zh: 昔趙之將帥多賈人，高帝懸之以重賞。

translations.literal: In olden times Zhao’s generals were mostly merchants; the High Ancestor hung out heavy rewards for them.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu once bought Zhao’s merchant-generals with gold—do the same.”

sentences.zh: 今西州新破，兵人疲饉，若招以財谷，則其眾可集。

translations.literal: Now the western province is newly broken; soldiers and people are weary and hungry; if you summon them with wealth and grain, then their multitudes can be gathered.
translations.idiomatic: Long is starving after defeat; offer pay and grain and the soldiers will flock in.”

sentences.zh: 臣知國家所給非一，用度不足，然有不得已也。

translations.literal: Your servant knows that what the state supplies is not one thing alone and expenditures are insufficient, yet there is what cannot be helped.”
translations.idiomatic: The treasury is thin, I know—but some costs cannot be shirked.”

sentences.zh: 』帝然之。

translations.literal: ” The emperor approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 於是大轉糧運，詔歙率征西大將軍馮異、建威大將軍耿弇、虎牙大將軍蓋延、揚武將軍馬成、武威將軍劉尚入天水，擊破公孫述將田弇、趙匡。

translations.literal: Thereupon he greatly shifted grain transport; he ordered Xi to lead the western expedition great general Feng Yi, the general who establishes might Geng Yan, the tiger-fang great general Gai Yan, the general who displays might Ma Cheng, and the general who displays martial power Liu Shang into Tianshui, striking and defeating Gongsun Shu’s generals Tian Yan and Zhao Kuang.
translations.idiomatic: He then poured supplies west and sent Lai Xi with Feng Yi, Geng Yan, Gai Yan, Ma Cheng, and Liu Shang into Tianshui, where they crushed Shu’s generals Tian Yan and Zhao Kuang.

sentences.zh: 明年，攻拔落門，隗囂支黨周宗、趙恢及天水屬縣皆降。

translations.literal: The next year he attacked and took Luomen; Wei Xiao’s collateral followers Zhou Zong and Zhao Hui together with the subordinate counties of Tianshui all surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: The next year they stormed Luomen; Zhou Zong, Zhao Hui, and every Tianshui district yielded.

sentences.zh: 初王莽世，羌虜多背叛，而隗囂招懷其酋豪，遂得為用。

translations.literal: In the beginning, in Wang Mang’s time, the Qiang barbarians for the most part rebelled, but Wei Xiao summoned and cherished their chieftains and stalwarts and thereby was able to employ them.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wang Mang the Qiang had risen; Wei Xiao had bought their chiefs and used them.

sentences.zh: 及囂亡後，五谿、先零諸種數為寇掠，皆營塹自守，州郡不能討。

translations.literal: When Xiao perished afterward, the various stocks of Wuxi and Xianling repeatedly acted as bandits and plundered; all walled themselves in moats, and the provinces and commanderies could not punish them.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiao died, Wuxi and Xianling tribes raided without end, each behind palisades the local troops could not crack.

sentences.zh: 歙乃大修攻具，率蓋延、劉尚及太中大夫馬援等進擊羌於金城，大破之，斬首虜數千人，獲牛羊萬餘頭，谷數十萬斛。

translations.literal: Xi thereupon greatly repaired siege equipment, led Gai Yan, Liu Shang, and grand counselor of the palace Ma Yuan and others to advance and strike the Qiang at Jincheng, greatly defeated them, beheaded and captured several thousand, obtained more than ten thousand head of cattle and sheep, and several hundred thousand hu of grain.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi rebuilt siege gear, marched with Gai Yan, Liu Shang, and Ma Yuan to Jincheng, shattered the Qiang, took thousands of heads, and captured vast herds and granaries.

sentences.zh: 又擊破襄武賊傅栗卿等。

translations.literal: Moreover he struck and defeated the bandits of Xiangwu such as Fu Liqing and others.
translations.idiomatic: He also broke Fu Liqing’s band at Xiangwu.

sentences.zh: 隴西雖平，而人饑，流者相望。

translations.literal: Although Longxi was pacified, the people were hungry, and refugees faced one another on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Longxi was quiet but famine sent refugees in endless lines.

sentences.zh: 歙乃傾倉廩，轉運諸縣，以賑贍之，於是隴右遂安，而涼州流通焉。

translations.literal: Xi thereupon emptied granaries, shifted transport to the various counties, to relieve and nurture them; thereupon the right of Long was pacified, and Liang province became linked in traffic.
translations.idiomatic: He emptied every granary and wagoned grain county by county until order returned to Longyou and trade reopened with Liangzhou.

sentences.zh: 十一年，歙與蓋延、馬成進攻公孫述將王元、環安於河池、下辨，陷之，乘勝遂進。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year Xi together with Gai Yan and Ma Cheng advanced to attack Gongsun Shu’s generals Wang Yuan and Huan An at Hanchi and Xiabian, took them, and riding victory pressed on.
translations.idiomatic: In year 11 he joined Gai Yan and Ma Cheng against Wang Yuan and Huan An at Hanchi and Xiabian, took both towns, and pressed the advantage.

sentences.zh: 蜀人大懼，使刺客刺歙，未殊，馳召蓋延。

translations.literal: The men of Shu were greatly afraid and sent an assassin to stab Xi; he was not yet dead; they galloped to summon Gai Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Shu in panic sent a dagger-man; the blow did not kill him at once, and messengers raced for Gai Yan.

sentences.zh: 延見歙，因伏悲哀，不能仰視。

translations.literal: When Yan saw Xi, he thereupon prostrated himself in grief and sorrow and could not look up.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Yan found him dying and wept face to the floor.

sentences.zh: 歙叱延曰：『虎牙何敢然！

translations.literal: Xi rebuked Yan, saying, “Tiger Fang—how dare you be thus!
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi snapped, “Tiger Fang—what is this sniveling?

sentences.zh: 今使者中刺客，無以報國，故呼巨卿，欲相屬以軍事，而反效兒女子涕泣乎！

translations.literal: Now the envoy has been struck by an assassin and has no means to repay the state; therefore I called you, Juqing, intending to entrust you with military affairs—yet you instead imitate children and women in weeping tears!
translations.idiomatic: I am dying for the realm and called you to hand over the army—must you whimper like a girl?”

sentences.zh: 刃雖在身，不能勒兵斬公耶！

translations.literal: Although the blade is in my body, can you not tighten the troops and strike the duke!”
translations.idiomatic: The knife is in my back—can you not rally the host and strike the foe?”

sentences.zh: 』延收淚強起，受所誡。

translations.literal: ” Yan gathered his tears, forced himself to rise, and received the admonition.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Yan swallowed his grief and took his orders.

sentences.zh: 歙自書表曰：『臣夜人定後，為何人所賊傷，中臣要害。

translations.literal: Xi personally wrote a memorial saying, “After the night’s still hour last night, your servant was wounded by someone’s attack; it struck your servant’s vital place.
translations.idiomatic: Lai Xi’s own memorial ran: “After midnight last night an assassin struck me in a mortal place.

sentences.zh: 臣不敢自惜，誠恨奉職不稱，以為朝廷羞。

translations.literal: Your servant does not dare pity himself; truly he hates that in performing duty he did not match it, thereby bringing shame on the court.
translations.idiomatic: I do not mourn my life—I mourn that I failed my charge and shamed Your Majesty.

sentences.zh: 夫理國以得賢為本，太中大夫段襄，骨鯁可任，願陛下裁察。

translations.literal: To order a state is to take obtaining the worthy as foundation; grand counselor of the palace Duan Xiang is firm of bone and fit to be employed; I beg Your Majesty to judge and examine.”
translations.idiomatic: The state rests on good men—Grand Counselor Duan Xiang is steadfast; I beg you to weigh his name.”

sentences.zh: 又臣兄弟不肖，終恐被罪，陛下哀憐，數賜教督。

translations.literal: Moreover your servant’s brothers are not worthy; in the end I fear they will incur guilt; Your Majesty in pity and compassion has repeatedly bestowed instruction and supervision.”
translations.idiomatic: My brothers are weak; I fear they will disgrace you—yet you have often schooled them; I beg your continued grace.”

sentences.zh: 』投筆抽刃而絕。

translations.literal: ” He cast aside the brush, drew the blade, and expired.
translations.idiomatic: He threw down his brush, drew the steel across his throat, and died.

sentences.zh: 帝聞大驚，省書攬涕，乃賜策曰：『中郎將來歙，攻戰連年，平定羌、隴，憂國忘家，忠孝彰著。

translations.literal: The emperor heard and was greatly startled; reading the memorial he gathered tears and thereupon bestowed a document saying, “Zhonglang general Lai Xi, in attack and battle year after year, pacified the Qiang and Long, forgot family for the state, loyalty and filial piety shining manifest.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wept over his letter and wrote: “Lai Xi of the zhonglang guard fought year on year, pacified Qiang and Long, forgot home for the state—his loyalty blazed.

sentences.zh: 遭命遇害，嗚呼哀哉！

translations.literal: Meeting fate and suffering harm—alas, how sad!”
translations.idiomatic: Cut down by treachery—alas for him!”

sentences.zh: 』使太中大夫贈歙中郎將、征羌侯印綬，謚曰節侯，謁者護喪事。

translations.literal: ” He sent a grand counselor of the palace to present Xi with the seals and ribbons of zhonglang general and marquis who conquers the Qiang, gave the posthumous title Marquis Jie, and a herald to supervise the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: He posthumously restored his rank as zhonglang general and marquis who conquers the Qiang, styled him Jie, and sent heralds to conduct the rites.

sentences.zh: 喪還洛陽，乘輿縞素臨吊送葬。

translations.literal: When the bier returned to Luoyang, the imperial carriage in plain white came to mourn and saw off the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: When the cortège reached Luoyang, the emperor in mourning white met the train.

sentences.zh: 以歙有平羌、隴之功，故改汝南之當鄉縣為征羌國焉。

translations.literal: Because Xi had merit in pacifying the Qiang and Long, therefore he changed Dangxiang county in Runan to be the state of Conquering-the-Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: For his western victories the court renamed Dangxiang in Runan “Conquering-the-Qiang.”

sentences.zh: 子褒嗣。

translations.literal: His son Bao succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Bao inherited.

sentences.zh: 十三年，帝嘉歙忠節，復封歙弟由為宜西侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year the emperor, praising Xi’s loyalty and constancy, again enfeoffed Xi’s younger brother You as marquis of Yixi.
translations.idiomatic: In year 13 the court also enfeoffed his brother You as marquis of Yixi for the family’s steadfast service.

sentences.zh: 褒子棱，尚顯宗女武安公主。

translations.literal: Bao’s son Ling married the Manifest Ancestor’s daughter, the senior princess of Wu’an.
translations.idiomatic: Bao’s son Ling wed Emperor Ming’s daughter, the Princess of Wu’an.

sentences.zh: 棱早歿，褒卒，以棱子歷為嗣。

translations.literal: Ling died early; when Bao died, Ling’s son Li was made heir.
translations.idiomatic: Ling died young; when Bao followed, Ling’s son Li carried the title.

sentences.zh: 論曰：『世稱來君叔天下信士。

translations.literal: The judgment says: “The age calls Lai Junshu the most trustworthy man under Heaven.”
translations.idiomatic: The summation says: men called Lai Junshu the most trusted man alive.

sentences.zh: 夫專使乎二國之間，豈厭詐謀哉？

translations.literal: Now to be sole envoy between two states—does one not weary of deceit and stratagem?
translations.idiomatic: Envoys between rival courts live by guile—

sentences.zh: 而能獨以信稱者，良其誠心在乎使兩義俱安，而己不私其功也。

translations.literal: Yet he alone could be praised for trustworthiness—truly it was because his sincere heart lay in making both sides’ right secure, while he himself did not privatize the merit.
translations.idiomatic: yet Lai Xi won fame for honesty because he sought peace for both camps, not glory for himself.

sentences.zh: 歷字伯珍，少襲爵，以公主子，永元中為侍中，監羽林右騎。

translations.literal: Li, styled Bozhen, in his youth inherited the title; because he was the princess’s son, in the Yongyuan period he became palace attendant and supervised the right wing of the feathered forest.
translations.idiomatic: Li Bozhen inherited young; as the princess’s son he became palace attendant under Yongyuan and commanded the right wing of the imperial guard.

sentences.zh: 永初三年，遷射聲校尉。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yongchu he was promoted to colonel of the shooting-sounds corps.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 3 he rose to colonel of the shooting-sounds corps.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，代馮石為執金吾。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongning he replaced Feng Shi as chief commandant of the guards.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongning 1 he replaced Feng Shi as chief commandant of the guards.

sentences.zh: 延光元年，尊歷母為長公主。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yanguang he honored Li’s mother as senior princess.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanguang 1 the emperor raised Li’s mother to senior princess.

sentences.zh: 二年，遷歷太僕。

translations.literal: In the second year he promoted Li to grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Li became grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 明年，中常侍樊豐與大將軍耿寶、侍中周廣、謝惲等共讒陷太尉楊震，震遂自殺。

translations.literal: The next year the palace attendant Feng Feng together with grand general Geng Bao, palace attendants Zhou Guang and Xie Yun and others jointly slandered and framed Grand Commandant Yang Zhen, and Zhen thereupon killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Then eunuch Feng Feng and Geng Bao framed Grand Commandant Yang Zhen, who took his own life.

sentences.zh: 歷謂侍御中虞詡曰：『耿寶托元舅之親，榮寵過厚，不念報國恩，而傾側奸臣，誣奏楊公，傷害忠良，其天禍亦將至矣。

translations.literal: Li said to imperial secretary Yu Xu, “Geng Bao, relying on kinship as maternal uncle, has favor and honor more than thick; he does not think to repay the state’s grace, but leans with treacherous ministers, falsely memorializes against Lord Yang, injuring the loyal and good—Heaven’s calamity too will soon reach him.”
translations.idiomatic: Li told Yu Xu, “Geng Bao’s power as the emperor’s uncle has swollen his pride; he joins villains to ruin Yang Zhen—Heaven will call him to account.”

sentences.zh: 』遂絕周廣、謝惲，不與交通。

translations.literal: Thereupon he broke with Zhou Guang and Xie Yun and did not communicate with them.
translations.idiomatic: He cut Zhou Guang and Xie Yun dead out of his circle.

sentences.zh: 時皇太子驚病不安，避幸安帝乳母野王君王聖舍。

translations.literal: At the time the heir apparent was alarmed and ill at ease in sickness and avoided favor to lodge at the house of Emperor An’s wet nurse, Lady Wang Sheng of Yewang.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince fell ill and moved into wet nurse Wang Sheng’s house at Yewang.

sentences.zh: 太子乳母王男、廚監邴吉等以為聖舍新繕修，犯土禁，不可久御。

translations.literal: The heir apparent’s wet nurse Wang Nan, kitchen intendant Bing Ji, and others thought that Sheng’s house was newly repaired and violated the earth taboo; one could not long dwell there.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Nan and Bing Ji warned that the new plaster broke earth taboos and the boy should not stay.

sentences.zh: 聖及其女永與大長秋江京及中常侍樊豐、王男、邴吉等互相是非，聖、永遂誣譖男、吉，皆幽囚死，家屬徙比景。

translations.literal: Sheng and her daughter Yong together with the grand prolonger of autumn Jiang Jing and palace attendants Feng Feng, Wang Nan, Bing Ji, and others mutually accused one another; Sheng and Yong thereupon falsely slandered Nan and Ji; all were imprisoned and died; family dependents were moved to Bijin.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Sheng and her daughter Yong traded charges with Jiang Jing and the eunuchs until Nan and Ji were jailed to death and their kin banished south.

sentences.zh: 太子思男等，數為嘆息。

translations.literal: The heir apparent thought on Nan and others and repeatedly sighed for them.
translations.idiomatic: The prince mourned Wang Nan and sighed again and again.

sentences.zh: 京、豐懼有後害，妄造虛無，構讒太子及東宮官屬。

translations.literal: Jing and Feng feared later harm and recklessly fabricated emptiness, constructing slander against the heir apparent and the eastern palace officials.
translations.idiomatic: Jing and Feng, fearing revenge, spun lies against the heir and his staff.

sentences.zh: 帝怒，召公卿以下會議廢立。

translations.literal: The emperor was angry and summoned dukes and ministers down to council on deposition and establishment.
translations.idiomatic: An enraged An summoned the high ministers to debate deposing the heir.

sentences.zh: 耿寶等承旨，皆以為太子當廢。

translations.literal: Geng Bao and others received the gist and all said the heir apparent ought to be deposed.
translations.idiomatic: Geng Bao and his clique echoed that the prince must go.

sentences.zh: 歷與太常桓焉、廷尉張皓議曰：『經說，年未滿十五，過惡不在其身。

translations.literal: Li together with Minister of Ceremonies Huan Yan and commandant of justice Zhang Hao deliberated, saying, “The classics say that before the age of fifteen is complete, faults are not on his person.
translations.idiomatic: Li joined Huan Yan and Zhang Hao: “The canon says a child under fifteen bears no guilt for others’ plots.

sentences.zh: 且男、吉之謀，皇太子容有不知，宜選忠良保傅，輔以禮義。

translations.literal: Moreover in the plotting of Nan and Ji, the heir apparent perhaps did not know; it is fitting to select loyal and good tutors and protectors and assist him with ritual and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: The wet nurses’ intrigue need not touch the boy—give him honest tutors instead.

sentences.zh: 廢置事重，此誠聖恩所宜宿留。

translations.literal: Deposition and establishment are weighty matters; this is truly what sagely grace ought to ponder overnight.
translations.idiomatic: Dethroning a crown prince deserves more than one night’s thought.”

sentences.zh: 』帝不從，是日遂廢太子為濟陰王。

translations.literal: The emperor did not follow; that same day he thereupon deposed the heir apparent and made him king of Jiyin.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused; that same day the prince became the king of Jiyin.

sentences.zh: 時監太子家小黃門籍建、中傅高梵等，皆以無罪徙朔方。

translations.literal: At the time the junior yellow gate Ji Jian who supervised the heir apparent’s household, tutor Gao Fan, and others, all without guilt were moved to Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: Household eunuch Ji Jian and tutor Gao Fan, though innocent, were exiled to Shuofang.

sentences.zh: 歷乃要結光祿勛祋諷，宗正劉瑋，將作大匠薛皓，侍中閭丘弘、陳光、趙代、施延，太中大夫朱倀、第五頡，中散大夫曹成，諫議大夫李尤，符節令張敬，持書侍御史龔調，羽林右監孔顯，城門司馬徐崇，衛尉守丞樂闈，長樂、未央廄令鄭安世等十餘人，俱詣鴻都門證太子無過。
