translations.literal: Li thereupon knotted ties with superintendent of the imperial household Dui Feng, director of the imperial clan Liu Wei, chief architect for the court Xue Hao, palace attendants Lüqiu Hong, Chen Guang, Zhao Dai, Shi Yan, grand counselors of the palace Zhu Chang and Diwu Kai, attendant censor Cao Cheng, grandee remonstrant Li You, tally marshal Zhang Jing, secretary censor with credentials Gong Diao, right supervisor of the feathered forest Kong Xian, gate colonel Xu Chong, assistant to the commandant of the guards Yue Kai, stable chiefs of Changle and Weiyang Zheng Anshi, and more than ten others, all going together to the Hongdu Gate to testify that the heir apparent had no fault.
translations.idiomatic: Li then rallied over a dozen high officials—from Dui Feng and Liu Wei to Gong Diao—to protest at the Hongdu Gate that the prince was blameless.

sentences.zh: 龔調據法律明之，以為男、吉犯罪，皇太子不當坐。

translations.literal: Gong Diao cited the law to clarify it, holding that in Nan and Ji’s crime the heir apparent ought not to sit guilty.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Diao cited statute: the wet nurses’ crime did not implicate the heir.

sentences.zh: 帝與左右患之，乃使中常侍奉詔脅群臣曰：『父子一體，天性自然。

translations.literal: The emperor together with those at his side worried over it; thereupon he sent a palace attendant bearing an edict to threaten the host of ministers, saying, “Father and son are one body; nature and heaven are spontaneous.”
translations.idiomatic: Panicked, the emperor sent eunuchs to menace the ministers: “Father and son are one flesh by nature.”

sentences.zh: 以義割恩，為天下也。

translations.literal: To sever with righteousness for the sake of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler may sever blood ties for the good of the realm.

sentences.zh: 歷、諷等不識大典，而與群小共為讙嘩，外見忠直而內希後福，飾邪違義，豈事君之禮？

translations.literal: Li, Feng, and others do not recognize the great norm but together with petty men make clamor; outwardly they appear loyal and straight yet inwardly hope for later blessing; they adorn evil and violate righteousness—is this the ritual of serving a lord?
translations.idiomatic: Li and his allies howl with nobodies, feigning loyalty while angling for favor—is that how one serves a lord?

sentences.zh: 朝廷廣開言事之路，故且一切假貸；

translations.literal: The court broadly opens the road for speaking on affairs; therefore for now everything is lent and pardoned;
translations.idiomatic: For now the court forgives this clamor once.

sentences.zh: 若懷迷不反，當顯明刑書。

translations.literal: If they cling to delusion and do not return, then the statutes of punishment shall be made clear.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who clings to delusion will face the full penalty of the law.

sentences.zh: 』諫者莫不失色。

translations.literal: The remonstrators none failed to change color.
translations.idiomatic: Every remonstrator turned pale.

sentences.zh: 薛皓先頓首曰：『固宜如明詔。

translations.literal: Xue Hao first knocked his head and said, “It is indeed fitting to act as the bright edict says.”
translations.idiomatic: Xue Hao was first to kowtow: “We must obey the edict.”

sentences.zh: 』歷怫然，廷詰皓曰：『屬通諫何言，而今復背之？

translations.literal: Li, flushed with anger, in the court rebuked Hao, saying, “When we passed the remonstrance, what words did we speak, yet now you again turn your back on them?
translations.idiomatic: Li flushed and shouted at him in open court: “What pact did we swear in the corridor—how dare you turn coat?”

sentences.zh: 大臣乘朝車，處國事，固得輾轉若此乎！

translations.literal: Great ministers ride the court carriage and occupy state affairs—may they indeed twist and turn thus!”
translations.idiomatic: Men who ride the state’s carriage should not flip like fish!”

sentences.zh: 』乃各稍自引起，歷獨守闕，連日不肯去。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon each gradually drew himself away and rose; Li alone guarded the gate and for successive days would not depart.
translations.idiomatic: The others slunk off; Li alone camped at the gate day after day.

sentences.zh: 帝大怒，乃免歷兄弟官，削國租，黜公主不得會見。

translations.literal: The emperor was greatly angry and thereupon dismissed Li and his brothers from office, cut the fief’s rent, and degraded the princess so she could not have audience.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor stripped the Li brothers, cut their income, and barred the princess from court.

sentences.zh: 歷遂杜門不與親戚通，時人為之震栗。

translations.literal: Li thereupon shut his gate and did not communicate with kin; people of the time for him trembled with fear.
translations.idiomatic: Li barred his doors to kin and left the capital in awe of him.

sentences.zh: 子定嗣。

translations.literal: His son Ding succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Ding inherited.

sentences.zh: 定尚安帝妹平氏長公主，順帝時，為虎賁中郎將。

translations.literal: Ding married Emperor An’s younger sister, the senior princess of the Ping clan; in Emperor Shun’s time he was leader of the gentlemen brave as tigers.
translations.idiomatic: Ding wed Emperor An’s sister, the Princess Ping; under Shun he led the tiger-brave guard.

sentences.zh: 定卒，子虎嗣，桓帝時，為屯騎校尉。

translations.literal: When Ding died, his son Hu succeeded; in Emperor Huan’s time he was colonel of garrison cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Ding’s son Hu became colonel of garrison cavalry under Huan.

sentences.zh: 弟艷，字季德，少好學下士，開館養徒，少歷顯位，靈帝時，再遷司空。

translations.literal: His younger brother Yan, styled Jide, in his youth loved learning and humbled himself before knights; he opened a hall and reared disciples; in his youth he passed through illustrious posts; in Emperor Ling’s time he was twice promoted to minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Yan, styled Jide, kept open house for scholars, rose through high office, and twice became minister of works under Ling.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：『李、鄧豪贍，舍家從讖。

translations.literal: The encomium says: “Li and Deng, rich and heroic, abandoned home to follow prophecy.
translations.idiomatic: The closing verse: Li and Deng staked fortune on a prophecy’s word—

sentences.zh: 少公雖孚，宗卿未驗。

translations.literal: Shaogong was indeed sincere, yet the director for the imperial clan was not verified.
translations.idiomatic: Shaogong spoke true, yet Mang’s astrologer-Liu was the wrong man.

sentences.zh: 王常知命，功惟帝念。

translations.literal: Wang Chang knew destiny; merit lay in the emperor’s remembrance.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang read Heaven’s mind and won the throne’s praise.

sentences.zh: 款款君叔，斯言無玷。

translations.literal: Earnestly earnest Junshu—those words knew no stain.
translations.idiomatic: Steadfast Junshu—his name stayed clean.

sentences.zh: 方獻三捷，永墜一劍。

translations.literal: Just as he offered three victories, forever he fell to one sword.
translations.idiomatic: Three triumphs won, one dagger ended him.”
